medications.indication_rationale: A thick layer of plain white soft paraffin over the plaques, ideally under a loose non-adherent dressing overnight, traps moisture, softens the hyperkeratotic scale and lets the fissures close — this is the definitive acute treatment for mechanical fissuring and directly stops the cracking-on-bending cycle. No prescription needed; safe on broken skin.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thick layer topically to the affected plaques, at least nightly (and after cleansing the fissures with saline or clean water), under a loose bandage/non-adherent dressing overnight

medications.drug_class: vitamin D analogue + topical corticosteroid combination
medications.indication_rationale: Resumption of the patient's specific antipsoriatic topical therapy addresses the underlying plaque disease that led to fissuring. It is appropriately (re)started at in-person GP review once the acute fissures have begun to heal — potent corticosteroid should not be driven into raw, bleeding, open fissures. No new dose is initiated remotely.
contraindications_in_this_case: avoid applying into open, actively bleeding fissures until the skin surface has re-epithelialised

medications.drug_name: oral or intravenous antibiotics (e.g. flucloxacillin) for presumed cellulitis
medications.indication_rationale: Systemic antibiotics are NOT indicated: the presentation is mechanical fissuring, not infection (afebrile, no purulence, non-spreading blanching rim, painless joint). Reflexively prescribing antibiotics — or sending the patient to ED for IV antibiotics — is the central error of commission this case tests. Antibiotics would be indicated ONLY if genuine infective red flags develop (see safety-netting).

medications.drug_name: topical antibiotic / antiseptic ointment to the fissures
medications.indication_rationale: There is no secondary infection to treat; a topical antibiotic adds sensitisation risk without benefit and is not part of managing clean mechanical fissures. Bland emollient occlusion is what is required.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not pick or peel off the silvery scales — this re-opens the cracks and causes bleeding.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Debriding the adherent scale mechanically worsens fissuring and bleeding.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the elbows continuously and heavily greased with a thick emollient and avoid bending the elbows sharply until the skin has softened.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintaining plaque pliability prevents re-fissuring on movement.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Resume regular use of the prescribed antipsoriatic topical therapy (as directed at GP review) rather than leaving the plaques untreated.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Treatment lapse allowed the plaques to thicken and dry to the point of fissuring; consistent therapy prevents recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Humidify the living environment (e.g. a room humidifier) during cold, dry weather.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Environmental dryness contributed to the plaques desiccating and cracking.

patient_education_points.point: The pain and bleeding are coming from dry cracks in the thickened skin, not from a bacterial infection; the surrounding redness is inflammation from the skin tearing and from rubbing.
Thick psoriasis plaques lose their stretch and split like dry leather when the joint bends — softening the plaque with heavy moisturiser and overnight occlusion is what heals the cracks.

This remote assessment cannot feel the skin or the joint, so it is presumptive — an in-person review is advised to physically confirm there is no infection, and you should seek care sooner if the warning signs below appear.
You do not need antibiotics or an emergency department visit for this at present; those are only needed if signs of true infection develop.

follow_up_plan.interval: prompt in-person GP review within 24–48 hours to physically exclude infection; then routine GP review at ~7 days to restart specific antipsoriatic topical therapy
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: palpate for induration/warmth/fluctuance and lymphangitis, assess the elbow joint, confirm the fissures are healing, and re-establish specific psoriasis treatment once the skin is intact
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any spreading redness up the arm, fever or chills, deep throbbing joint pain, or purulent/yellow-green discharge — seek urgent care before the scheduled review

must_include_items: Recognise the most likely diagnosis as benign mechanical fissuring of known psoriatic plaques, not infection
Actively screen the infective red flags (fever, spreading/tracking redness, purulence, deep joint pain)
Advise emollient/occlusive wound care (thick soft paraffin under overnight occlusion; do not pick the scale)
Acknowledge the telehealth limit — palpation and joint exam cannot be done remotely — and arrange prompt in-person review to physically exclude cellulitis/septic bursitis
Provide clear conservative safety-netting for when to escalate
acceptable_alternatives: Advising a same-day in-person review instead of 24–48 h if the AI judges residual uncertainty higher
Recommending a generic thick emollient/ointment in place of white soft paraffin
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for the infective red flags at all
Failing to safety-net for spreading redness, fever, deep joint pain or purulence
Failing to recognise that remote exclusion of cellulitis is presumptive and that in-person review is warranted
errors_of_commission: Prescribing or recommending oral/IV antibiotics for presumed cellulitis
Directing the patient to the emergency department for IV antibiotics
Diagnosing cellulitis / septic joint with false certainty and managing as infection
Instructing the patient to drive potent topical corticosteroid into open bleeding fissures
Telling the patient to pick or debride the silvery scale
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both (a) NOT over-treating as infection (no antibiotics, no ED-for-IV-antibiotics) AND (b) NOT dismissing without safety-netting and in-person review given telehealth cannot palpate. Emollient occlusive wound care plus conservative safety-netting is the model answer.
